1: wĕhamelek Dāwîḏ zāqēn bā bayāmîm wĕlō yiḥam lô: 'And king David was old, advanced in days, and he could not get warm.' Two phrases mark the narrative's opening: zāqēn bā bayāmîm (old, come in days) — the standard biblical idiom for advanced age (Genesis 18:11: Abraham and Sarah; Genesis 24:1: Abraham alone; Joshua 13:1; 1 Kings 23:1-2: Joshua) — and the verb yiḥam (Qal imperfect of ḥmm, to be warm), here negated: the king can no longer maintain body heat. This is the standard biblical onset-of-death marker; its significance is immediately political: a king who cannot maintain physical warmth cannot exercise executive authority.
2: naʿărâ ḇĕṯûlâ: 'a young woman, a virgin.' The two terms are distinct: naʿărâ describes a young woman of marriageable age (the same word used of Rebekah in Gen 24 and Ruth gleaning in Ruth 2); ḇĕṯûlâ (H1330) specifies virginal status. The proposal is therapeutic — ANE medicine recognized the warming effect of body heat proximity for the elderly — not sexual. Verse 4 makes explicit that David 'did not know her' (wĕhammelek lō yĕḏāʿāh), a formulaic Hebrew idiom for no sexual intercourse (Genesis 4:1: Adam 'knew' Eve; 1 Samuel 1:19). The proposal comes from David's servants (ʿăḇāḏāyw), not from David himself.
3: wayĕḇaqqĕšû naʿărâ yāp̄â bĕḵōl gĕḇûl yiśrāʾēl: 'They searched for a beautiful young woman throughout all the territory of Israel.' The search is a national operation — kol gĕḇûl yiśrāʾēl (the whole border/territory of Israel) is a comprehensive formula for the entire kingdom. Šûnēm (Shunem) is in the tribal territory of Issachar, in the Valley of Jezreel — the same village where the Shunammite woman will live in 2 Kings 4 (Elisha raises her son). The geographic precision is characteristic of the court archive sources underlying 1-2 Kings.
4: wattihî lammmelek mĕšāreṯeṯ: 'she became his attendant/servant.' The verb šārat (Piel, to serve/minister) is used for the service of Levites in the sanctuary (Numbers 1:50) and for personal household service; it denotes a close attendant relationship, not a wife or concubine. The emphatic statement wĕhammelek lō yĕḏāʿāh legally and morally clarifies her status: Abishag is never David's wife or concubine. This distinction will have political consequences in 1 Kings 2, when Adonijah's request for Abishag is interpreted as a claim on the throne.
5: wayyiṯnasĕʾ ʾăḏōniyyāhû: 'Adonijah exalted himself.' The verb is Hitpael of nāśāʾ (H5375) — reflexive/intensive, 'he lifted himself up.' The same pattern is used for Absalom (2 Samuel 15:1: wayyaʿaś lô ʾAḇšālôm reḵeḇ ûp̄ārāšîm, chariot and horsemen). The Absalom-Adonijah parallel is structural: the third and fourth sons both attempt to seize the throne through public display and faction-building while the father lives. ʾĂnî ʾemlōk: 'I will be king' — the declarative self-assertion before any divine or royal authorization, which is the narrative's definition of the illegitimate seizure.
6: wĕlō-ʿăṣāḇô ʾāḇîw miyyāmāyw lēʾmōr maddûaʿ kāḵâ ʿāśîṯā: 'And his father had not disciplined him at any time by saying, Why have you done this?' The verb ʿāṣaḇ (H6087) means to distress, grieve, or restrain — here it describes parental correction. The same parental failure appears in Eli (1 Samuel 3:13: his sons did evil and he did not restrain them) and David himself with Amnon (2 Samuel 13:21: David was angry but did not discipline him). The note that Adonijah was 'also very handsome in appearance' (gam-hûʾ ṭôḇ tōʾar mĕʾōḏ) echoes Absalom (2 Samuel 14:25); external beauty without inner character creates the same trajectory.
7: wayyihyû dĕḇārāyw ʿim Yôʾāḇ ben-Ṣĕrûyâ wĕʿim ʾAḇyāṯār hakkōhēn: 'His agreements/words were with Joab son of Zeruiah and with Abiathar the priest.' Dĕḇārāyw (his words/matters) here means his political negotiations or compact — an agreement to support his claim. Both Joab and Abiathar are key figures: Joab has been David's military commander throughout the Samuel narrative but has resisted David's authority repeatedly (killing Abner, Amasa, Absalom against David's explicit wishes); Abiathar is the priest from Eli's line (1 Samuel 22:20) who has served David since Nob. Their alignment with Adonijah represents the older guard's faction.
8: wĕṢāḏôq hakkōhēn ûḇĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ wĕNāṯān hannaḇîʾ wĕŠimʿî wĕRēʿî wĕhaggibōrîm ʾăšer lĕḏāwîḏ: Those NOT with Adonijah. The counter-faction is significant: Zadok (priest from the Eleazar line, not Eli's Abiathar line), Benaiah (commander of the elite corps), Nathan the prophet (David's court prophet since 2 Sam 7), and the gibbōrîm (David's professional warriors). The Zadok-Abiathar division prefigures the resolution of 1 Kings 2:26-27: when Abiathar is expelled, Zadok alone remains, establishing the Zadokite priestly line that will serve the Jerusalem temple through the monarchy.
9: wayyzaḇaḥ ʾAḏōniyyāhû ṣōʾn ûḇāqār ûmĕrîʾ ʿim ʾeḇen hazzōḥeleṯ: 'Adonijah sacrificed sheep, oxen, and fattened cattle at the stone of Zoheleth.' The sacrificial feast (zāḇaḥ) near En-rogel (the spring at the junction of the Kidron and Hinnom valleys, southeast of Jerusalem, outside the city) is a political maneuver: the sacrificial feast would be interpreted as a covenant meal ratifying Adonijah's kingship among his supporters. The invited list is explicitly political: 'all the king's sons and all the men of Judah, the king's servants.'
10: wĕʾeṯ-Nāṯān hannaḇîʾ wĕʾeṯ-Bĕnāyāhû wĕʾeṯ-haggibbōrîm wĕʾeṯ-šĕlōmōh ʾāḥîw lōʾ qārāʾ: 'But Nathan the prophet, Benaiah, the mighty men, and Solomon his brother he did not invite.' The deliberate exclusion of these figures from the covenant meal is the political signal: Adonijah is constituting his faction and excluding the rival faction. The exclusion of Solomon specifically marks this as a succession move. The narrator names them here to highlight the symmetry: those excluded by Adonijah are exactly those who will execute Solomon's succession.
11: wayĕdabbēr Nāṯān ʾel-Baṯ-šeḇaʿ ʾem šĕlōmōh: Nathan goes first to Bathsheba. Nathan's stratagem (vv11-14) is political wisdom: approach the king through Bathsheba rather than directly, creating a personal and maternal stake. The phrase hălōʾ šāmaʿt ('have you not heard?') opens with the rhetorical question of urgent news. Nathan's characterization of Adonijah's rule as threatening to both Bathsheba and Solomon — 'that both you and Solomon will be counted as criminals (ḥaṭṭāʾîm)' — is the political leverage: she has a survival reason to act, not merely a promise to invoke.
12: wĕʿattâ lĕkî ʾîṣĕʿēḵ nā ʿēṣâ: 'Now let me give you counsel.' ʿĒṣâ (H6098, counsel, advice) is the key political wisdom term throughout Samuel-Kings (Ahithophel's ʿēṣâ in 2 Sam 16-17 was 'like the word of God'). Nathan's counsel is tactically sound: have Bathsheba make the appeal on personal grounds (the king's oath) and Nathan will follow to confirm. The two-witness structure (Deuteronomy 19:15: a matter must be established by two or three witnesses) gives the appeal maximum legal weight.
13: hălōʾ ʾattâ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek nišbaʿtā lăʾămaṯĕkā lēʾmōr kî šĕlōmōh binkā yimlōk ʾaḥărāy: 'Did you not, my lord the king, swear to your handmaid saying, Your son Solomon shall reign after me?' The oath invoked here has no prior narrative documentation in 2 Samuel — it is presupposed as historical fact. The formula nišbaʿtā (Niphal perfect of šāḇaʿ, to swear) is the standard covenant-oath verb. Bathsheba's self-designation as ʾămaṯĕkā (your female slave/handmaid) is the formal court address of petition — it invokes covenant obligation, not merely personal relationship.
14: hinnēh ʿôḏēḵ mĕḏabbereṯ šām ʿim-hammelek wĕʾanî ʾāḇôʾ ʾaḥărāyiḵ: 'Behold, while you are still speaking there with the king, I will come in after you.' Nathan's timing is precise — Bathsheba makes the initial petition, creating the emotional and legal grounds, and Nathan follows to confirm. The sequential structure (woman first, prophet second) maximizes the impact: a mother's personal stake, then prophetic confirmation. This is political strategy by those who believe they are acting in accordance with God's choice.
15: waṯābōʾ Baṯ-šeḇaʿ ʾel-hammelek haḥăḏrâ: 'Bathsheba came to the king in the inner chamber.' The ḥeḏer (inner chamber) is the most private room — David is being tended by Abishag. The scene underscores David's physical diminishment: the king who was once the most dynamic military and political figure in Israel now lies in the inner chamber unable to keep warm. Bathsheba enters, prostrates herself (waṯiqqōḏ waṯišṯaḥû lammmelek), and waits for him to ask her purpose.
16: waṯiqqōḏ Baṯ-šeḇaʿ waṯišṯaḥû lammmelek: 'Bathsheba bowed and prostrated herself to the king.' The verb pair qāḏad (to bow the head) + šāḥâ (to prostrate oneself) is the royal obeisance formula — used of subjects before kings and of worshipers before YHWH. By contrast, when Bathsheba later comes before Solomon in v16 (ch 1 Kings 2:19), he rises and bows to her — a reversal that demonstrates Solomon's honor to his mother and the changed political reality.
17: wayĕdabbēr ʾaḏōnî ʾeloyhēkā YHWH ʾel-ʾămaṯĕkā lēʾmōr: The invocation of 'YHWH your God' (using the third person to refer to the Lord) in Bathsheba's petition elevates the claim from personal request to covenantal obligation. David's oath was sworn by YHWH (ḥay YHWH, 'as YHWH lives', v29 confirms this formula) — breaking it would be not merely political failure but covenant perjury. The appeal grounds Solomon's succession in divine promise, not human political maneuvering.
18: wĕʿattâ hinnēh ʾAḏōniyyāhû mālaḵ: 'And now, behold, Adonijah has become king.' The perfect tense mālaḵ — 'has become king,' presenting it as an accomplished fact rather than ongoing process — is the rhetorical framing of urgency. Bathsheba presents the succession as a fait accompli that David must reverse, not a plan he can prevent. The appeal to David's ignorance ('you did not know it') may be slightly overstated — the narrative does not say David was completely unaware of Adonijah's actions — but politically it grants David the face-saving option of acting without acknowledging that he had passively allowed the situation to develop.
19: wayyzaḇaḥ šôr ûmĕrîʾ wĕṣōʾn lārōḇ wayyiqrāʾ lĕḵol bĕnê hammmelek: 'He sacrificed oxen and fattened cattle and sheep in abundance and invited all the king's sons.' The repetition of the feast-detail from v9 is Bathsheba's report to David — the narrative deliberately repeats the information to show what David was being told. The phrase wĕlišĕlōmōh ʿaḇdĕkā lōʾ qārāʾ (and Solomon your servant he did not call) is the pivot of the petition: Solomon's exclusion is the evidence that the succession is being stolen, and it personalizes the abstract political claim.
20: wĕʾattâ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek ʿênê kol-yiśrāʾēl ʿāleykā lĕhaggîḏ lāhem mî yēšēḇ ʿal-kissēʾ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek ʾaḥărāyw: 'And now, my lord the king, the eyes of all Israel are upon you, to tell them who will sit on the throne of my lord the king after him.' The phrase ʿênê kol-yiśrāʾēl ʿāleykā (the eyes of all Israel are upon you) is a political and covenantal pressure statement — the king has a public obligation to declare his succession. 'Eyes upon' is a standard idiom for expectant attention requiring response. The question is not merely dynastic but concerns the stability of the covenant community.
21: wĕhāyâ kĕšeḵeḇ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw wĕhāyîṯî ʾanî ûḇĕnî šĕlōmōh ḥaṭṭāʾîm: 'And it will be, when my lord the king lies with his fathers, that I and my son Solomon will be regarded as criminals.' The idiom šāḵaḇ ʿim ʾăḇōṯāyw (to lie with one's fathers) is the standard death formula for Israelite kings throughout 1-2 Kings — it will appear dozens of times. Ḥaṭṭāʾîm (sinners/criminals) — those who have violated the law. Bathsheba frames the political danger in the sharpest terms: if Solomon is passed over, she and Solomon will be treated as rebels against the new king.
22: wĕhinnēh ʿôḏennāh mĕḏabbereṯ ʿim-hammelek wĕNāṯān hannaḇîʾ bāʾ: 'And while she was still speaking with the king, Nathan the prophet came.' The narrative timing — Nathan arrives precisely while Bathsheba is speaking — is either coordinated (as Nathan told her in v14) or presented as providentially timed. The two-witness structure is completed: Bathsheba makes the petition, Nathan arrives to confirm it. The narrator uses the same phrasing as Nathan's instruction to create a ring structure confirming the plan worked exactly as devised.
23: wayuggad lammmelek lēʾmōr hinnēh Nāṯān hannaḇîʾ: Nathan is formally announced to the king — the court announcement protocol. His entry after Bathsheba follows the sequence Nathan prescribed; his presence confirms that both advisors are aligned on the matter. The parallel announcement formulas (Bathsheba prostrating in v16, Nathan prostrating in v23 with wayyišṯaḥû lammmelek ʾappāyw ʾarṣâ) create a literary balance: two witnesses, two prostrations, one message.
24: wayyōʾmer Nāṯān ʾăḏōnî hammelek ʾattâ āmartā ʾAḏōniyyāhû yimlōk ʾaḥărāy wĕhûʾ yēšēḇ ʿal-kissĕʾî: Nathan's approach is indirect — he does not say 'you promised Solomon.' Instead he asks: 'Did you say Adonijah shall reign?' This rhetorical strategy forces David to either deny or affirm, without Nathan presuming to accuse the king. The rhetorical question (ʾattâ āmartā) invites David to repudiate Adonijah's kingship on his own initiative rather than at Nathan's instruction.
25: kî yāraḏ hayyôm wayyizbaḥ šôr ûmĕrîʾ wĕṣōʾn lārōḇ: 'For today he has gone down and sacrificed oxen and fattened cattle and sheep in abundance.' The third repetition of the feast-detail (vv9, 19, 25) drives the point into David's consciousness. The phrase wayyōmal wayyēšt lĕp̄ānāyw wayyōmĕrû yĕḥî hammmelek ʾAḏōniyyāhû (and they are eating and drinking before him and saying, Long live King Adonijah!) mimics the exact formula that will be used for Solomon (v39: yĕḥî hammmelek šĕlōmōh) — the same formal proclamation formula, applied to the wrong king. Nathan is dramatizing the urgency.
26: wĕlî ʾanî-ʿaḇdĕkā wĕlĕṢāḏôq hakkōhēn ûlĕḇĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ ûlišĕlōmōh ʿaḇdĕkā lōʾ qārāʾ: The list of those not invited corresponds exactly to the faction listed in v8 as loyal to Solomon. Nathan articulates his own exclusion alongside Zadok, Benaiah, and Solomon — placing himself in the company of the legitimate succession supporters. The self-description as ʿaḇdĕkā (your servant) throughout these verses is court protocol, not merely deference; it signals loyal covenant-servant status.
27: ʾim mēʾēṯ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek nihyâ haddāḇār hazzeh: 'Was this thing done by my lord the king?' The question is designed to force David's hand: either the succession was deliberately given to Adonijah (in which case Nathan and the others need to know) or it was not (in which case David must act). Nathan's closing wĕlōʾ hôḏaʿtā ʾeṯ-ʿaḇdĕkā mî yēšēḇ ʿal-kissēʾ ʾăḏōnî-hammelek ʾaḥărāyw (and you have not informed your servant who would sit on the throne) is deniably accusatory — you left us in the dark — pressing for explicit royal declaration.
28: wayyaʿan hammmelek Dāwîḏ wayyōʾmer qirĕʾû-lî lĕḇaṯ-šeḇaʿ: 'King David answered and said, Call Bathsheba to me.' David's response is immediate — he calls Bathsheba back in (she had presumably left when Nathan arrived) and delivers his oath. The scene pivots: David moves from passivity (lying in the inner chamber unable to get warm) to decisive royal action. The oath formula he pronounces (v29-30) is the most formal possible: sworn by YHWH, invoking his own redemption from adversity.
29: ḥay YHWH ʾăšer pāḏâ ʾeṯ-nap̄šî mikkol-ṣārâ: 'As YHWH lives, who has redeemed my soul from all adversity.' The oath formula ḥay YHWH (as YHWH lives) is the standard Hebrew oath-swearing formula, invoking YHWH as the living guarantor of the pledge. The relative clause — 'who has redeemed my soul from all adversity' — makes the oath deeply personal: it invokes David's entire history of divine deliverance (the theme of 2 Sam 22 = Psalm 18, where YHWH is the one who rescued David from all enemies). The verb pāḏâ (to redeem by paying a price, H6299) is the covenantal redemption term, distinct from gāʾal (kinsman-redeemer).
30: kî kaʾăšer nišbaʿtî lāḵ bĕYHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr kî-šĕlōmōh binkā yimlōk ʾaḥărāy: 'Just as I swore to you by YHWH the God of Israel saying, Solomon your son shall reign after me.' The oath is now confirmed — nišbaʿtî (I swore) + the specific content + the formula kî ḵēn ʾeʿĕśeh hayyôm hazzeh (so I will do this day). The immediate execution ('this day') is the characteristic urgency of royal decree in the Samuel-Kings narrative: when the king decides, action follows without delay. The three witnesses to this re-confirmation of the oath are Bathsheba, Nathan, and implicitly Abishag who is present.
31: waṯiqqōḏ Baṯ-šeḇaʿ ʾappāyw ʾarṣâ waṯišṯaḥû lammmelek waṯōʾmer yĕḥî ʾăḏōnî hammmelek Dāwîḏ lĕʿōlām: 'Bathsheba bowed with her face to the ground and prostrated herself to the king and said, May my lord King David live forever!' The proclamation yĕḥî hammmelek (long live the king!) + his name + lĕʿōlām (forever) is the formal royal acclamation. Bathsheba's response is the formal court expression of gratitude and loyalty. The lĕʿōlām (forever) is formulaic courtesy, not a literal claim of immortality — similar expressions appear throughout the ancient Near East as royal acclamation formulae.
32: wayyōʾmer hammmelek Dāwîḏ qirĕʾû-lî lĕṢāḏôq hakkōhēn ûlĕNāṯān hannaḇîʾ ûlĭḇĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ: David summons the three key actors for Solomon's anointing: Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah the military commander. The triad represents the three institutions of legitimate authority: priestly, prophetic, and military. This is the institutional support structure that legitimizes the succession. Notably absent: Joab (the military commander who went with Adonijah) and Abiathar (the priest who supported Adonijah). The succession is constituted by the replacement faction.
33: wĕhirkabtem ʾeṯ-binkā šĕlōmōh ʿal-happirĕḏâ ʾăšer-lî: 'Have my son Solomon ride on my own mule.' The king's personal mule (pirĕḏâ) is the most important detail of the anointing procession: in the ancient Near East, riding the king's personal animal was equivalent to a public declaration of succession. David's mule is the physical symbol of royal authority being transferred. Compare Absalom's mule in 2 Samuel 18:9 — Absalom was riding 'the king's mule' when he got caught in the oak tree, suggesting that claiming the royal mule was already part of the succession-claim protocol.
34: ûmāšaḥ ʾōṯô šām Ṣāḏôq hakkōhēn wĕNāṯān hannaḇîʾ lĕmelek ʿal-yiśrāʾēl: 'And let Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet anoint him there as king over Israel.' The double anointing — priest and prophet together — is unprecedented in the biblical narrative; David was anointed by Samuel alone (1 Samuel 16:13), and Saul was anointed by Samuel alone (1 Samuel 10:1). The double anointing of Solomon by both priest and prophet underscores the institutional legitimacy of his succession: priestly sanction and prophetic confirmation simultaneously. The wĕqĕraʾtem qôl haššôp̄ār (blow the shofar) announces the proclamation publicly.
35: ûḇāʾtem ʾaḥărāyw wĕḇāʾ wĕyāšaḇ ʿal-kissĕʾî ûhûʾ yimlōk taḥtāy: 'Come after him, and he shall come and sit on my throne, for he shall be king in my place.' The formula ûhûʾ yimlōk taḥtāy (he shall reign in my place) — taḥtāy (H8478) literally 'under me' or 'in my stead' — is the formal succession declaration. David appoints Solomon in his own place while still living: a co-regency arrangement. The appointment of Solomon over both Israel and Judah (lĕmelek ʿal-yiśrāʾēl wĕʿal-yĕhûḏâ) — listing both — signals the pan-Israelite scope of the succession.
36: wayyaʿan Bĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ ʾeṯ-hammelek wayyōʾmer ʾāmēn kēn yōʾmar YHWH: Benaiah's response — ʾāmēn (so be it, verily) + kēn yōʾmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê ʾăḏōnî hammelek (may YHWH, the God of my lord the king, say so) — is one of the earliest uses of ʾāmēn as a liturgical affirmation in the Hebrew Bible. The term derives from the root ʾmn (firmness, faithfulness, reliability) and affirms the content of what has been declared. Benaiah's response constitutes the military institution's formal assent to the succession.
37: kaʾăšer hāyâ YHWH ʿim-ʾăḏōnî hammmelek kēn yihyeh ʿim-šĕlōmōh: 'As YHWH was with my lord the king, so may he be with Solomon.' Benaiah's blessing invokes the YHWH-with formula (YHWH ʿimmaḵā) — the covenant-presence language that runs through the entire Deuteronomistic History (Joshua 1:5: YHWH promises to be with Joshua as with Moses; 1 Samuel 18:12, 14: YHWH was with David). The explicit wish that Solomon receive the same divine presence that David had is theologically loaded — it acknowledges that David's success was due to divine accompaniment, and petitions the same for the new king. The continuation — wîgaḏdēl ʾeṯ-kissĕʾô miqqissĕʾ ʾăḏōnî hammmelek Dāwîḏ (and make his throne greater than the throne of my lord King David) — is the succession-blessing formula.
38: wayyēreḏ Ṣāḏôq hakkōhēn wĕNāṯān hannaḇîʾ ûḇĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ wĕhakkĕrēṯî wĕhappĕlēṯî: 'So Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet and Benaiah son of Jehoiada and the Cherethites and the Pelethites went down.' The kĕrēṯî ûp̄ĕlēṯî (Cherethites and Pelethites) are David's elite foreign bodyguard — mercenaries of Philistine/Aegean origin who formed the royal personal guard throughout David's reign (2 Samuel 8:18; 1 Kings 15:18; 1 Kings 20:7, 23). Their presence in Solomon's anointing procession signals military continuity and the professional army's allegiance to the new king.
39: wayyiqqaḥ Ṣāḏôq hakkōhēn ʾeṯ-qereṇ haššemen mēhāʾōhel wayyimšaḥ ʾeṯ-šĕlōmōh: 'Zadok the priest took the horn of oil from the tent and anointed Solomon.' The qereṇ haššemen mēhāʾōhel (horn of oil from the tent) specifies the sacred anointing oil from the tabernacle — the tent David had set up for the Ark in Jerusalem (2 Samuel 6:17). The use of tabernacle oil connects Solomon's anointing to the Mosaic tradition: the same holy anointing oil (šemen hamišḥâ, Exodus 30:23-33) used to consecrate the tabernacle and Aaron's priestly line is used to consecrate the Davidic king. The ram's horn as vessel for the anointing oil echoes Samuel's anointing of David (1 Samuel 16:13 uses a qāren horn).
40: wayiqqĕʿû baššôp̄ār wayyōmĕrû kol-hāʿām yĕḥî hammmelek šĕlōmōh: 'They blew the shofar and all the people said, Long live King Solomon!' The šôp̄ār (ram's horn trumpet) was the standard instrument for public royal proclamations, military signals, and religious assemblies. Its sounding announces the succession to the entire city and surrounding area. The tricolon ʿōlîm ʾaḥārāyw kol-hāʿām mĕḥallĕlîm baḥălîlîm ûśĕmēḥîm śimḥâ gĕḏôlâ (all the people came up after him playing flutes and rejoicing with great joy) is a festal joy-procession formula — the city is given over to celebration. Ḥălîlîm (flutes/pipes) appear in festal contexts throughout the OT.
41: wayyišmaʿ ʾAḏōniyyāhû wĕḵol-haqqĕrûʾîm ʾăšer ʾittô wĕhēm killû leʾĕkōl: 'Adonijah and all the guests who were with him heard it, and they had just finished eating.' The dramatic irony is pointed: Adonijah's coronation feast is interrupted mid-celebration by the sound of Solomon's anointing procession. The phrase wattirʿaš hāʿîr (the city was in uproar) describes the overwhelming public noise of Solomon's celebration reaching Adonijah's feast at En-rogel outside the city. Joab's question — 'What is this noise of the city in uproar?' — is the moment Adonijah's faction realizes their bid has failed.
42: ʿôḏennû mĕḏabbēr wĕhinnēh Yônāṯān ben-ʾAḇyāṯār hakkōhēn bāʾ: 'While he was still speaking, behold, Jonathan son of Abiathar the priest came.' The messenger Jonathan son of Abiathar (not Jonathan the son of Saul) is the bearer of catastrophic news for Adonijah's faction. Adonijah's greeting — bōʾ kî ʾîš ḥayil ʾattâ ûṭôḇ tĕḇassēr (come, for you are a valiant man and bring good news) — uses the root bśr (to bring news, especially good/victory news), the same root as bĕśôrâ and NT euangelion (gospel). Adonijah hopes for a good-news report; he receives the opposite.
43: wayyaʿan Yônāṯān wayyōʾmer lĕʾAḏōniyyāhû ʾāḵēn ʾăḏōnênû hammmelek Dāwîḏ himmĕlîḵ ʾeṯ-šĕlōmōh: 'Jonathan answered Adonijah, Truly/indeed our lord King David has made Solomon king.' The adverb ʾāḵēn (truly, indeed, on the contrary) is a strong affirmative/adversative particle that signals the news will overturn expectations. The perfect himmĕlîḵ (has made king) is the accomplished fact. Jonathan's report is a detailed account of the anointing procession — emphasizing all the institutional actors (Zadok, Nathan, Benaiah, the Cherethites/Pelethites) to establish the legitimacy of what has occurred.
44: wayišlaḥ ʾittô hammelek ʾeṯ-Ṣāḏôq hakkōhēn wĕʾeṯ-Nāṯān hannaḇîʾ ûḇĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ: The messenger repeats the three institutional actors in v44 — king sent with him (Solomon) Zadok, Nathan, Benaiah. The repetition of the institutional triumvirate in the messenger's report mirrors the original commission in v32-34, creating a narrative confirmation that the succession happened exactly as David commanded. The narrative structure itself validates the succession's legitimacy.
45: wayyimšaḥû ʾōṯô Ṣāḏôq hakkōhēn wĕNāṯān hannaḇîʾ lĕmelek bĕGîḥôn: 'And Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet have anointed him king at Gihon.' Gihon (Gîḥôn) is the spring on the eastern slope of the City of David, the primary water source for Jerusalem. Its selection as anointing site is significant: water-source ceremonies in the ancient Near East had sacred associations, and Gihon's prominence in Jerusalem's water system (later connected to Hezekiah's tunnel) made it a natural public gathering point. The proclamation and public celebration at Gihon allows the news to spread immediately through the city.
46: wĕgam yāšaḇ šĕlōmōh ʿal kissēʾ hammamlāḵâ: 'And moreover Solomon sits on the royal throne.' The verb yāšaḇ (to sit) in the context of 'sitting on the throne' is the standard formula for enthroned royal authority — the king 'sits' as a technical expression for exercising judicial and executive power (Psalms 9:4: 'you sat on the throne judging righteously'). The messenger's declaration — Solomon is already seated on the throne — is the final proof that the coup attempt has completely failed and Adonijah's faction must immediately recalibrate.
47: wĕgam bāʾû ʿaḇdê hammelek lĕḇārēḵ ʾeṯ-ʾăḏōnênû hammmelek Dāwîḏ: 'And moreover the king's servants came to bless our lord King David.' The royal servants' blessing formula — yĕhî ʾĕlōheykā ʾeṯ-šēm šĕlōmōh ṭôḇ miššĕmeḵā (may your God make the name of Solomon better than your name) — is the court acclamation that the new king's name will be even greater than the old king's. The response of David — wayyišṯaḥû hammmelek ʿal-hammiššāh (and the king bowed himself on the bed) — is the physical act of worship: David, lying in bed, bows in gratitude to YHWH. The physical diminishment (he can only bow while lying) makes the act of thanksgiving more poignant.
48: bārûḵ YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer nāṯan hayyôm yōšēḇ ʿal-kissĕʾî wĕʿênay rōʾôṯ: David's blessing — 'Blessed is YHWH God of Israel who has given one to sit on my throne today while my own eyes see it.' The formula bārûḵ YHWH is the standard Hebrew blessing doxology — giving YHWH the credit for what has occurred. The phrase wĕʿênay rōʾôṯ (and my own eyes seeing it) is the personal testimony of witnessed fulfillment — David has lived to see the covenant promise fulfilled. The Davidic covenant of 2 Sam 7 promised that a son of David would succeed him on the throne; this is the beginning of that fulfillment.
49: wayyeḥĕrĕḏû wayyāqûmû kol-haqqĕrûʾîm ʾăšer lĕʾAḏōniyyāhû wayyēlĕḵû ʾîš lĕḏarKô: 'All the guests who were with Adonijah trembled and rose and each went his own way.' The verb ḥāraḏ (to tremble, shudder) describes the sudden terror of Adonijah's supporters when they realize the coup has failed. The immediate dispersal — 'each went his own way' — is the dissolution of the political faction. In ANE political culture, association with a failed claimant to the throne was potentially capital; the guests' flight is self-preservation. The rapid dispersal demonstrates that Adonijah's faction had no deep commitment — it was opportunistic alignment with an apparently successful pretender, not a principled political movement.
50: wĕʾAḏōniyyāhû yārēʾ mippĕnê šĕlōmōh wayyāqom wayyēleḵ wayyaḥăzēq bĕqarnôṯ hammizbēaḥ: 'Adonijah feared Solomon and rose and went and took hold of the horns of the altar.' The altar sanctuary was the traditional place of refuge — Exodus 21:13-14 permits the accidental manslayer to flee to a 'place' (later understood as the altar), but explicitly excludes the premeditated murderer. The altar horns (qarnôṯ hammizbēaḥ) are the four raised corners of the altar that the priest touched with the blood of sin offerings (Leviticus 4:18; Exodus 30:10) — grasping them was the equivalent of grabbing sanctuary. Adonijah's action is an appeal for mercy: he puts himself in the space of divine mercy.
51: wayuggad lišĕlōmōh lēʾmōr hinnēh ʾAḏōniyyāhû yārēʾ ʾeṯ-hammmelek šĕlōmōh: 'It was told Solomon, Behold, Adonijah fears King Solomon.' The report reaching Solomon frames Adonijah's altar-clinging as fear of Solomon personally rather than appeal for divine mercy — a politically accurate reading. Adonijah sends his request through intermediaries: yiššāḇaʿ-lî kayyôm hammmelek šĕlōmōh ʾim-yahărōg ʾeṯ-ʿaḇdô baḥereb (let King Solomon swear to me today that he will not kill his servant with the sword). The request is for a formal oath of safety, not merely informal mercy.
52: wayyōʾmer šĕlōmōh ʾim yihyeh lĕben-ḥayil lōʾ-yippōl misśaʿărāṯô ʾarṣâ: 'Solomon said, If he shows himself worthy (ben-ḥayil), not one of his hairs will fall to the earth.' Ben-ḥayil (son of valor/worth) is the standard term for a man of quality and loyalty. Solomon's conditional pardon is precise: ongoing loyalty will bring safety; wickedness will bring death. This is the conditional covenant formula applied to the political situation — the same structure as the conditional Davidic covenant (1 Kings 2:4: 'if your sons take heed to their way'). Solomon's mercy is real but not unconditional.
53: wayyišlaḥ hammmelek šĕlōmōh wayyōrîḏûhû mēʿal hammizbēaḥ wayyāḇōʾ wayyišṯaḥû lammmelek šĕlōmōh: 'King Solomon sent and they brought him down from the altar, and he came and bowed before King Solomon.' Adonijah's prostration before Solomon — wayyišṯaḥû lammmelek šĕlōmōh — is the formal submission of the defeated claimant to the victorious king. Solomon's dismissal — lēḵ lĕḇêṯeḵā (go to your house) — is the royal command sending Adonijah under informal house arrest, a mercy compared to execution. The chapter ends with Adonijah alive because of Solomon's conditional mercy — a mercy Adonijah will subsequently abuse (ch 1 Kings 2:13-25).
